(C) Solution Apparatus/Chemicals Separating funnel, conical or cylindrical Glass funnel Beaker Conical flask Glass rod Filter paper NaOH or KOH solution Anhydrous Na2SO4 or MgSO4 Procedure Transfer the impure kerosene into a separating funnel using a glass/simple funnel. ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day)

You went to a Fuel Service Station to buy a litre of kerosene but you were informed that the stock available was contaminated with some benzoic acid. You need the kerosene to perform an experiment so you are forced to buy the impure one with the intention of purifying it. You cannot do a distillation but you can purify it by another method of your choice. How would you purify the kerosene? Provide a list of all materials including chemicals needed for the purification. (C)
Solution
Apparatus/Chemicals
Separating funnel, conical or cylindrical
Glass funnel
Beaker
Conical flask
Glass rod
Filter paper
NaOH or KOH solution
Anhydrous Na2SO4 or MgSO4
Procedure
Transfer the impure kerosene into a separating funnel using a glass/simple funnel.
Add about 100 cm3 of NaOH or KOH solution to the kerosene.
Stopper the funnel and shake vigorously.
Drain out the aqueous layer and add fresh NaOH or KOH solution.
Shake and drain the aqueous layer.
Wash the kerosene about three times, each time with about 50 cm3 of water.
Drain the kerosene into a conical flask.
Add anhydrous Na2SO4 or MgSO4 until the solid can move freely when the flask is swirled around.
Filter the kerosene into a suitable, clean container through a fluted filter paper and stopper the container.

questionText: Q. I am a graph of some sort. Rectangles are my main feature. Associated with me are class boundaries and class marks. I am used to represent data and to be precise I am a graph of a frequency distribution. My name suggests I am part of history and part of a diagram. Who am I?

subject: Physics
questionText: Q. In the 20th century, geneticists who were interested in determining the chemical nature of the genetic material began to work with me. I have two types of life cycles, termed lytic and lysogenic cycles. Even though my name sounds like bacteria I am rather a virus. My name literally means 'eater of bacteria', because I attack bacteria. One type of bacteria readily infected by me is E. coli which lives in the mammalian large intestine. Who am I?

(P) A. 20 N [ ] ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (B & P) In order to determine the effect of high altitude on the performance of two groups of athletes, sprinters and endurance athletes, a competition was organized for the two groups of athletes at a place of higher altitude. Explain how high altitude will affect the performance of the athletes. Solution As one moves up a higher altitude, the air becomes thinner. Thinner air means less resistance, so the sprinters are expected to perform better at high altitude. However the thinner air also means reduction in the amount of oxygen, hence the pace of the endurance athletes which depends on high rates of oxygen consumption gets slower at high altitude. ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (B & P)

In order to determine the effect of high altitude on the performance of two groups of athletes, sprinters and endurance athletes, a competition was organized for the two groups of athletes at a place of higher altitude. Explain how high altitude will affect the performance of the athletes.
Solution

As one moves up a higher altitude, the air becomes thinner. Thinner air means less resistance, so the sprinters are expected to perform better at high altitude. However the thinner air also means reduction in the amount of oxygen, hence the pace of the endurance athletes which depends on high rates of oxygen consumption gets slower at high altitude. This is negligible for the spirit athletes since all the oxygen needed for the short dash is already in the muscle and bloodstream.

Hybridization ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (C) You have been provided all the apparatus needed to prepare a column for column chromatograph to separate two coloured compounds. Describe how the column will be set up. Solution Procedure The glass column is washed thoroughly with detergent, rinsed and dried in the oven at 110-120°C. The column is clamped upright, on a retort stand. A small plug of cotton or glass wool is pushed down to the bottom of the column. The column is filled with petroleum ether (boiling range 60-80°C). A suitable quantity of the adsorbent is weighed in a beaker. A small volume of petroleum ether is added and the mixture stirred to give a slurry. Using a funnel, and while stirring, the slurry is added, small quantities at a time to the column whose tap is left open. The column is gently tapped with the glass rod to prevent any air bubble being formed or trapped. When all the slurry has been added, there should be some solvent on top of the adsorbent. The tap is now closed until the column is about to be used. ROUND THREE (True or False) Q. The electric field of two equal charges has maximum magnitude at the centre of the line joining the charges. ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (C)
You have been provided all the apparatus needed to prepare a column for column chromatograph to separate two coloured compounds. Describe how the column will be set up.
Solution
Procedure
The glass column is washed thoroughly with detergent, rinsed and dried in the oven at 110-120°C.
The column is clamped upright, on a retort stand.
A small plug of cotton or glass wool is pushed down to the bottom of the column.
The column is filled with petroleum ether (boiling range 60-80°C).
A suitable quantity of the adsorbent is weighed in a beaker.
A small volume of petroleum ether is added and the mixture stirred to give a slurry.
Using a funnel, and while stirring, the slurry is added, small quantities at a time to the column whose tap is left open.
The column is gently tapped with the glass rod to prevent any air bubble being formed or trapped.
When all the slurry has been added, there should be some solvent on top of the adsorbent.
The tap is now closed until the column is about to be used.

questionText: Q. My name is in two parts. One is that of a container and the other in chemistry refers to the basic substances that make up matter. I am typically found in angiosperms but absent from most gymnosperms such as the conifers. At maturity, my protoplast dies and disappears, but my lignified cell walls persist. I am one of the cell types found in xylem which is the water conducting tissue of plants. Who am I?

Q. A body will be weightless in a space vehicle which is accelerating at a rate g in a region of outer space where gravitational forces are negligible. (P)
A. False
Q. Since the reduction potential for the couple Hg2+/Hg is +0.80V, metallic mercury should discharge hydrogen gas from a mineral acid. (C)
A. False
Q. An infinite set can be placed in a one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers. (M)
A. True
Q. A set is finite if it is countable. (M)
A. False
Q. Mammals and birds have fully developed double circulation. (B)
A. True
Q. The newton (N) is a base unit in the SI system. (B)
A. False
Q. During the charging of lead-acid storage battery, oxygen and hydrogen are also formed. (C)
A. True
Q. Iron is the most abundant element in the earth's crust. (C)
A. False [Oxygen is the most abundant element]
Q. A number is divisible by 15 if it is divisible by both 3 and 5. (M)
A. True
Q. Fishes have fully developed double circulation. (B)
A. False
Q. The Becquerel (Bq) is a derived unit in the SI system. (P)
A. True
Q. The mass of deuterium, a stable isotope of hydrogen containing a proton and a neutron, is less than the sum of proton and neutron masses. (C)
A. True [The difference represents the binding energy of neutron and proton in deuterium]
Q. The mass of oxygen in reasonably pure air is about 50% of the total mass. (C)
A. False []
Q. There is a finite set of prime numbers. (M)
A. False [Set of prime numbers is infinite]
Q. Collenchyma cells are common in herbaceous stems and roots. (B)
A. False
Q. The cell walls of fungi have cellulose. (B)
A. False [They have chitin]
ROUND FOUR (Riddles)

Q. I am a process encountered both in the laboratory and in industry. I join many small molecules together to form giant molecules. In nature, formation of natural rubber is a carbon copy of my industrial process. I charge no fees so nothing is taken away from the constituent molecules. One of my industrial end-products has turned out to be a major pollutant. Nylon is made not through me.

subject: Chemistry
questionText: Q. Name the odd one, and give a reason. Parenchyma; Collenchyma; Sclerenchyma; Epidermis (B) A. Epidermis (It is only complex tissue) Q. Name the odd one, and give a reason. Xylem; Phloem; Epidermis; Periderm (B) A. Periderm (It is the only secondary tissue/absent in the primary plant body) Q. The measures of the angles of a triangle are x°, 2x° and 3x°. Find x. (M) A. x=30 [] Q. The measures of the angles of a triangle are 60°, x° and 3x°. Find x. (M) A. x=30 [] Q. Why does an electric transformer hum? (P) A. Expansion and contraction of the magnetic core generates sound. Q. What is the hum frequency of an AC mains transformer, if the mains frequency is 60 Hz? (P) A. 120 Hz [Maximum expansion occurs at the extremum of each half cycle] Q. State the nature of the bonding between water molecules and Na2CO3 in washing soda. (C) A. Na2CO3.10H2O attractive forces, no chemical bond. Q. The blue solution of copper ions in dilute ammonia has the complex cation [Cu(NH3)4]2+. What is the nature of the bonding between Cu2+ and NH3? (C) A. Dative covalent bonds Q. Name the primary meristem that is responsible for producing collenchyma cells. (B) A. Ground meristem/Periblem Q. Name the primary meristem that is responsible for producing sieve tube elements. (B) A. Procambium/Plerome Q. An item is on sale at 25% off the original price. If the sale price is GH¢ 150, find the original price. (M) A. GH¢ 200 [] Q. An item priced at GH¢ 150 is sold for GH¢ 129 during a reduction sale. Find the % reduction. (M) A. 14 % [] Q. Why are coastal areas warm in the evening and cool during the day compared to more remote regions? (P) A. The specific heat capacity of ocean water is higher than that of rock, so in the evening, a warm breeze from heat stored in the ocean during the day circulates to land to warm air on the ocean surface rises and sinks on land to replace cold air blowing to sea to replace the rising warm air; during the day, the land is warmer and the warm air over it rises to be replaced by cooler air from the sea Q. Why are static electricity effects more pronounced during the harmattan season than during the rainy season? (P) A. Water is a conductor of electricity so moisture tends to dissipate built up static charge; in the harmattan season, there is little moisture in the atmosphere Q. Using your knowledge of relative reactivities of metals, what do you expect to see if a strip of aluminium metal is dripped into a solution of Cu2+ ions? (C) A. Copper will be deposited on the Al Q. In which medium will the solubility of calcium hydroxide be higher, pure water or sodium hydroxide solution? (C) A. Pure water () [In NaOH, OH- will support forward reaction] Q. Name the vitamin whose absence causes a deficiency disorder called pellagra. (B) A. Vitamin B3/Nicotin acid/niacin Q. Name the vitamin whose absence causes a deficiency disorder called xeropthalmia. (B) A. Vitamin A/Retinol Preamble: Evaluate the function for the given value of. (M) Q. A. [] Q. A. [] Q. What physical quantity now forms the basis for the definition of the meter? (P) A. The speed of light in a vacuum [The speed of light in a vacuum is defined to be 299792458 ms-1 exactly. The meter is defined as the distance travelled in vacuum by light in] Q. What physical quantity now forms the basis for the definition of the second? (P) A. The radiation emitted when Cs makes a transition from the first excited state to the ground state [The second is now defined as the time for 9192361770 oscillations of the radiation emitted when Cs makes a transition from the first excited state to the ground state] Q. What physical parameter of matter is a direct measure of the average kinetic energy of the constituent units (atoms/molecules/ions)? (P) A. Temperature Q. When the air in a flying balloon is heated, the balloon rises. Why? (P) A. With increase of the temperature of the air, the pressure of the air increases/ density of the air is decreased, thus increasing the buoyancy of the balloon Q. Name the specialised type of bone that surrounds the roots of teeth. (B) A. Cement Q. What is the name of the outermost layer of the tooth? (B) A. Enamel Preamble: A family has 2 children. The birth of a boy or a girl are equally likely. (M) Q. Find the probability that both children are of the same sex. A. [] Q. Find the probability both children are of different sexes. A. [] Q. Why does a boomerang not follow the parabolic path of a projectile but can return to the thrower? (P) A. The surfaces of the boomerang are shaped in such a way that lift can be generated as it flies through the air Q. Why should the discus be thrown against the wind? (P) A. Aerodynamic lift is then greatest, the discus remains airborne longer and it covers a longer distance Q. Arrange the following products of crude oil in increasing order of boiling point: petrol, diesel oil and aviation fuel. (C) A. petrol<aviation fuel<diesel oil Q. Name three types of hydrocarbons found in crude oil. (C) A. alkanes, cycloalkanes and aromatics ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (B) Study specimens A and B carefully and answer the following questions. Identify fully specimens A and B. Give one obvious reason each to support your answer. State three observable adaptable features in specimen A for feeding by the animal. State three observable adaptive features in specimen B for feeding by the animal. Solution Specimen A - Lower jaw bone of a herbivore Specimen B - Lower jaw bone of carnivore Reasons: Specimen A - Presence of Diastema Specimen B - Presence of well-developed canine teeth (carnassial teeth) Three observable adaptable features of specimen A to its diet Sharp (Chisel-like) incisors for cutting vegetation (Leaves/food) Ridged surface of molars and premolars for grinding/chewing food. Diastema provides space for the tongue to turn food during chewing. (manipulation of food bolus keeps chewed grass separate from fresh grass) Three observable adaptable features of specimen B to its diet The incisor teeth are chisel shaped for removing scraps of meat from bones (Nibbling/gripping) Prominent curved and pointed canine teeth to pierce the prey and hold it firmly. Molars and premolars have sharp and pointed surfaces for crushing bones and cutting the meat into smaller pieces. ROUND THREE (True or False) Q. The nitrogen in the ammonia molecule and in the ammonium ion have the same hybridisation. (C) A. True [both sp3] Q. If in a molecule there are 4 atoms around a central atom, the shape of the molecule would be tetrahedral. (C) A. False [square planar is possible] Q. The most abundant element in the earth's crust is oxygen. (C) A. True Q. The second most abundant element in the earth's crust is silicon. (C) A. True Q. Tadpoles have external gills for exchange of gases. (B) A. True Q. Most tadpoles are carnivorous. (B) A. False [herbivorous] Q. Forest trees have thicker leaves than savanna trees. (B) A. False Q. Leaves of savanna trees have blunt tips whereas those of forest trees have long drip tips. (B) A. True Q. If two angles are supplementary then they cannot both be acute. (M) A. True Q. For an even function, the graph is symmetrical about the y-axis. (M) A. True Q. Every quadratic function has an inverse. (M) A. False Q. In a trapezium, opposite sides are parallel. (M) A. False [a pair of opposite sides are parallel] Q. A string suspended from a moving body and carrying a mass at its lower end will be inclined to the vertical if the body is moving with constant speed. (P) A. False Q. A string suspended from a moving body and carrying a mass at its lower end will be inclined to the vertical at a large angle if the speed of the body is large. (P) A. False Q. An object initially at rest in a closed box will rise to the top if the box falls freely from rest. (P) A. False Q. A passenger in a lift (an elevator) accelerating downwards will rise to the top of the lift (elevator). (P) A. True ROUND FOUR (Riddles) Q. I was born in Shewsbury, England in 1809. As a boy I showed a keen interest in natural history, which was in difference to my physician father's wishes. I enrolled in the University of Edinburgh medical to study medicine. I later studied theology at Cambridge University but could not excel in either subject. In spite of that I am known for a voyage around the world which lasted for 5 years. The theory of evolution by natural selection is emphasised in my book, the Origin of Species which was published in 1859. Who am I?

ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (B)
Study specimens A and B carefully and answer the following questions.
Identify fully specimens A and B.
Give one obvious reason each to support your answer.
State three observable adaptable features in specimen A for feeding by the animal.
State three observable adaptive features in specimen B for feeding by the animal.
Solution
Specimen A - Lower jaw bone of a herbivore
Specimen B - Lower jaw bone of carnivore
Reasons:
Specimen A - Presence of Diastema
Specimen B - Presence of well-developed canine teeth (carnassial teeth)
Three observable adaptable features of specimen A to its diet
Sharp (Chisel-like) incisors for cutting vegetation (Leaves/food)
Ridged surface of molars and premolars for grinding/chewing food.
Diastema provides space for the tongue to turn food during chewing. (manipulation of food bolus keeps chewed grass separate from fresh grass)
Three observable adaptable features of specimen B to its diet
The incisor teeth are chisel shaped for removing scraps of meat from bones (Nibbling/gripping)
Prominent curved and pointed canine teeth to pierce the prey and hold it firmly.
Molars and premolars have sharp and pointed surfaces for crushing bones and cutting the meat into smaller pieces.

Q. I was born in Shewsbury, England in 1809. As a boy I showed a keen interest in natural history, which was in difference to my physician father's wishes. I enrolled in the University of Edinburgh medical to study medicine. I later studied theology at Cambridge University but could not excel in either subject. In spite of that I am known for a voyage around the world which lasted for 5 years. The theory of evolution by natural selection is emphasised in my book, the Origin of Species which was published in 1859.

subject: Biology
questionText: Q. I am a class of organisms. The study of my members is called batrachology. Though my members typically have four limbs, some are noted for being limbless. There is a dramatic decline in the population of my members which is perceived as one of the most critical threats to global biodiversity. My name literally means life on both sides. My members are superficially similar to reptiles. Some of my members include the salamanders, toads, and frogs. Who am I?

Despite my strength I am extremely allergic to heat as I soften and glow. If I was born at a piggery, then Who am I?

Q. A charge moves in uniform electric and magnetic fields which are parallel. What will be the trajectory of the charge if it is initially moving in the direction of the electric field? (P)
A. Straight line parallel to the electric field [the magnetic force is zero]
Q. A charge moves in a uniform magnetic field. What will be the trajectory of the charge if it is initially moving at an angle to the field? (P)
A. Helix with axis parallel to the field [There is no force in the direction of the field, only a force of constant magnitude perpendicular to the velocity component perpendicular to the field]

ROUND TWO (Problem of the Day) (C)

You are given a white, powdered inorganic solid, which is supposed to be an intimate mixture of Na2CO3 and NaHCO3 but the proportions are not known. The task is to determine these proportions by a volumetric analysis.
List all the reagents, glassware and other apparatus needed for the analysis
Describe how you would proceed to do the analysis
Write the chemical equations for the reactions that take place
Indicate the volumes/titres that will be used in calculating the relative proportions.
Solution
Apparatus and Reagents
Weighing bottle
Spatula
250 cm3 Volumetric flask
250 cm3 Conical flasks
Funnel
Beaker, 50 or 100 cm3
Pipette 10 or 20 or 25 cm3
Burette 20 or 25 cm3
Standardised HCl solution approximately 0.10 moldm-3
Indicators, phenolphthalein and methyl orange
De-ionised or distilled water
Procedure
Weigh a calculated mass of the solid using the weighing bottle (w1)
Transfer the weighed solid to a beaker and weigh the bottle again (w2)
Dissolve the solid in de-ionised or distilled water
Transfer the solution into the volumetric flask using the funnel
Make the solution up to the mark with more water
Shake the flask to obtain a homogeneous solution
Pipette 10 or 20 or 25 cm3 of the solution and titrate it against the standardised HCl solution using phenolphthalein as indicator
Repeat until at least 2 consistent titres are obtained; average titre V1
Repeat the titration using methyl orange as indicator, average titre V2
Chemical equations
With phenolphthalein
With methyl orange
Volumes to be used:
